results.title: The Landscape of Agentic Reinforcement Learning for LLMs: A Survey
results.content:
## 2 Preliminary: From LLM RL to Agentic RL

LLMs are initially pre-trained using behavior cloning, which applies maximum likelihood estimation (MLE) to static datasets such as web-scraped text corpora. Subsequent post-training methods enhance capabilities and align outputs with human preferences‚Äîtransforming them beyond generic web-data replicators. A common technique is supervised fine-tuning (SFT), where models are refined on human-generated (prompt, response) demonstrations. However, procuring sufficient high-quality SFT data remains challenging. Reinforcement fine-tuning (RFT) offers an alternative by optimizing models through reward functions, circumventing dependence on behavioral demonstrations. [...] The prospective horizon for agentic planning lies in the synthesis of these two paradigms: moving beyond the distinction between external search and internal policy optimization. The ultimate goal is to develop an agent that internalizes the structured search process itself, seamlessly blending intuitive, fast plan generation with deliberate, slow, deliberative reasoning. In such a model, RL would not only refine the final plan but also optimize a meta-policy governing the deliberation process: learning when to explore alternative paths, how to prune unpromising branches, and how deeply to reason before committing to an action. This would transform the LLM agent from a component that either proposes actions or acts as a raw policy into an integrated reasoning engine.

### 3.2 Tool Using

results.title: Reinforcement Learning vs Agentic AI: Two Different Meanings
results.content: Understanding ‚ÄòReinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards‚Äô (RLVR) As AI moves from experimental to enterprise-ready, one of the persistent challenges is how to train models reliably, transparently and for business outcomes. Traditional reinforcement-learning setups often rely on human labels, proxy metrics or learned reward models ‚Äî all of which introduce ambiguity and risk. RLVR offers an alternative: rule-based feedback in domains where correctness can be verified (think math, code, certain decision-tasks) and a binary reward signal (e.g., correct/incorrect). Why should marketing & business executives care? Because: - It improves model trustworthiness and governance. - It lowers the barrier between proof-of-concept and production-scale deployment. - It can sharpen alignment [...] The word ‚Äúreinforcement‚Äù shows up in both Reinforcement Learning and Agentic AI ‚Äî but it means two very different things. In Reinforcement Learning (RL), reinforcement is literal. An agent interacts with its environment, gets rewards or penalties, and adjusts its behavior. Just like training a dog with treats, the feedback loop reinforces the right actions over time. In Agentic AI, reinforcement is metaphorical. Here, it refers to reinforced capabilities ‚Äî reasoning, planning, memory, and tool use. Instead of learning from numeric rewards, the system is ‚Äúreinforced‚Äù with extra abilities that let it act autonomously, like a smart assistant with access to calendars, emails, and apps. üëâ RL = reinforcement through feedback signals. üëâ Agentic AI = reinforcement through augmented intelligence [...] üëâ Agentic AI = reinforcement through augmented intelligence and autonomy. Same word, but two very different paths to making AI smarter

results.title: From Reasoning to Agentic: Credit Assignment in Reinforcement ...
results.content:
The absence of intermediate verifiability means that PRM-style methods, which are the most established approach in reasoning RL, cannot be directly transferred to agentic settings. This gap drives the development of alternative approaches: hindsight-based credit (Tan et al., 2026) (evaluate actions after seeing outcomes), implicit credit via DPO (Liu et al., 2025) (avoid explicit step-level evaluation entirely), and privileged critics (Zhou et al., 2025) (use information available only at training time to provide step-level signals).

### 4.6 Challenge 6: The Bifurcation Point Problem [...] AgentPRM adapts the PRM paradigm from reasoning to agentic settings by replacing MC-based step labeling with TD+GAE-based value estimation. The key insight is that MC labeling‚Äîsampling continuations from each step to estimate step correctness‚Äîis prohibitively expensive in agentic settings because it requires re-executing environment interactions (spinning up sandboxed environments, making real API calls, etc.). Instead, AgentPRM trains a step-level critic using temporal difference learning: V‚Äã(st)‚ÜêV‚Äã(st)+Œ±‚Äã[rt+Œ≥‚ÄãV‚Äã(st+1)‚àíV‚Äã(st)]V(s\_{t})\leftarrow V(s\_{t})+\alpha[r\_{t}+\gamma V(s\_{t+1})-V(s\_{t})], with GAE for advantage estimation. Applied to tool-use, code generation, and web navigation tasks, AgentPRM reports 8√ó8\times better sample efficiency compared to MC-based PRM training. This

results.title: What is Agentic Reinforcement Learning? Full Guide with Examples & Use Cases
results.content:
Traditional reinforcement learning, especially in language models, often works in a single-step setup. The model receives an input, generates an output, and gets a reward. That‚Äôs it. There is no ongoing interaction or evolving state. Agentic RL, on the other hand, introduces multi-step decision-making. The agent interacts with an environment, updates its understanding, and continuously refines its strategy. This shift allows AI to move beyond static responses into dynamic problem-solving.

#### Key Differences [...] Agentic Reinforcement Learning is an advanced form of reinforcement learning where an AI system behaves like an agent,it takes actions, observes outcomes, and adjusts its behavior over time to achieve a goal. Unlike traditional systems that generate a single response, agentic systems operate in loops. They plan multiple steps, interact with tools, and adapt based on feedback. In simple terms, the AI is no longer just answering,it is doing. [...] Scenario: A regional trade disruption blocks a primary shipping route.
 Agent Action: The system automatically identifies alternative suppliers via API, recalculates the ‚ÄúTotal Cost of Ownership,‚Äù and re-routes shipments.
 The RL Edge: Agents are rewarded for balancing speed vs. cost, allowing them to navigate geopolitical volatility better than static scripts.

### 3. ‚ÄúAdaptive Middleware‚Äù in Education

The 2026 shift in EdTech is moving from ‚ÄúContent‚Äù to ‚ÄúOutcomes.‚Äù

 Pattern: Agents act as a ‚ÄúPredictive Safety Net,‚Äù identifying students likely to drop out 3‚Äì4 weeks before they actually do.
 Action: The agent dynamically pivots the curriculum‚Äîif a student struggles with a theory, it generates a practical case study in an industry they like (e.g., Renewable Energy).

results.title: The Landscape of Agentic Reinforcement Learning for LLMs: A Survey
results.content: Abstract: The emergence of agentic reinforcement learning (Agentic RL) marks a paradigm shift from conventional reinforcement learning applied to large language models (LLM RL), reframing LLMs from passive sequence generators into autonomous, decision-making agents embedded in complex, dynamic worlds. This survey formalizes this conceptual shift by contrasting the degenerate single-step Markov Decision Processes (MDPs) of LLM RL with the temporally extended Partially Observable Markov Decision Processes (POMDPs) that define Agentic RL. Building on this foundation, we propose a comprehensive twofold taxonomy: one organized around core agentic capabilities, including planning, tool use, memory, reasoning, self-improvement, and perception, and the other around their applications across
